1: parakletos (parakletos) — advocate, intercessor, one who is called to stand alongside — applied here to Christ as the heavenly advocate (pros ton patera, before the Father). This is the same title given to the Holy Spirit in John 14:16,26; 15:26; 16:7. The double Paraclete-theology: the Spirit as earthly advocate (present with the community) and Christ as heavenly advocate (interceding at the Father's right hand). Iesous Christos dikaios — Jesus Christ the righteous — his righteousness is the ground of his advocacy; he intercedes not on our merit but on the basis of his own righteous character and completed atonement.
2: hilasmos (hilasmos) — propitiation. The Greek term (from hilaskomai) carries in LXX usage the sense of satisfying divine wrath by an appointed means (cf. hilasterion = mercy seat in Exod 25:17, Rom 3:25). John uses the word twice (here and 4:10) — not the weaker expiation (removal of guilt alone) but the full propitiation (turning away wrath through sacrifice). peri holes tes kosmou — for the whole world — the scope is universal in offer while the community possesses the reality through faith.
18: antichristos (antichristos) — a NT coinage, not found in LXX or earlier Greek literature, appearing only in 1-2 John. Anti carries both against and in place of — the antichrist is both the opponent of Christ and the counterfeit claimant to Christ's role. The surprising claim is polloi antichristoi gegonasin — many antichrists have come — the historical manifestation of the spirit of antichrist in false teachers is the intensification and precursor of the singular eschatological figure, not a separate category. estin hote eschatou hora — it is the last hour — the eschatological urgency is grounded in this proliferation.
20: chrisma (chrisma) — anointing, the noun from chriein (to anoint), same root as Christos. The community has received an anointing from the Holy One — the Spirit as the christological anointing now shared with all believers (the democratization of the messianic anointing, fulfilling Joel 2:28). oidate pantes — you all know — the Spirit-anointing produces universal knowledge of divine truth within the community, not an elite gnosis reserved for some. This is the ground for distinguishing truth from error without needing outside authority.
27: to chrisma ho elabete — the anointing you received — aorist, a past definitive reception, now remaining (menei, present tense). The indwelling Spirit-anointing is the community's permanent possession and teacher. didaskei hymas peri panton — teaches you about all things — the Spirit's scope is comprehensive doctrinal formation. alethe estin kai ouk estin pseudos — it is true and is no lie — the Spirit's testimony is epistemically certain, contrasted with the lies of the false teachers whose spirit is deceptive.
3: ἐγνώκαμεν αὐτόν — perfect of γινώσκω: "we have come to know him" — a completed past event (the initial knowing) whose result (the ongoing knowledge) stands. This contrasts with the claim of v.4 (ἔγνωκα αὐτόν, also perfect but boasted without corresponding behavior). Both verses use the same form; the difference lies in the behavioral evidence John requires. ἐὰν τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ τηρῶμεν — τηρέω (G5083) = to guard, maintain, observe — not merely to obey once but to hold in custody. Present subjunctive implies habitual keeping.
4: ψεύστης — "a liar." The word is blunt and judicial. In 1 John, ψεύστης is applied to those who deny the Son (2:22), those who claim to love God while hating their brother (4:20), and here to those whose verbal claim of knowing Christ is contradicted by behavior. The category is not "mistaken" but "falsifying." ἡ ἀλήθεια ἐν τούτῳ οὐκ ἔστιν — truth as a living indwelling reality, absent from this person (cf. 1:8 and 1:10).
5: ὃς δ᾽ ἂν τηρῇ αὐτοῦ τὸν λόγον — "but whoever keeps his word." The shift from ἐντολάς (commandments, plural, vv.3-4) to λόγον (word, singular) is significant: keeping the word is keeping the total message, not merely rule-following. ἀληθῶς ἐν τούτῳ ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ τετελείωται — "the love of God has been truly perfected in this person." Τετελείωται: perfect passive of τελειόω — "has been brought to its goal." Ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ — the genitive is probably subjective (God's love reaching its intended expression in us), though both readings are grammatically possible.
6:
ὁ λέγων ἐν αὐτῷ μένειν — "the one who says he abides in him." Μένω (G3306) in 1 John is not static dwelling but dynamic staying — choosing continually to remain in the relationship. The one who claims this abiding ὀφείλει... περιπατεῖν — "is obligated to walk." Ὀφείλω (G3784) is a term of moral debt: not "it would be nice" but "owes it."

καθὼς ἐκεῖνος περιεπάτησεν — "just as that one walked." The Greek ἐκεῖνος (that one) is a demonstrative pronoun John uses specifically for Jesus in elevated, almost confessional contexts (3:3,5,7,16; 4:17). The aorist περιεπάτησεν summarizes Jesus' entire earthly life as a completed pattern for imitation.
7: ἀγαπητοί (agapētoi) — "Beloved." The word is the adjective form of ἀγάπη, meaning those who are loved — not merely friends by social circumstance but those within the embrace of covenant love. ἐντολὴν παλαιάν — παλαιός (G3820) = old in the sense of long-standing, ancient. The old command (love one another) dates from "the beginning" — probably the beginning of the community's reception of the gospel, not creation. The old command is the message they heard — obedience is tied to the original proclamation.
8: ἐντολὴν καινήν — the same commandment is also new. Καινός (G2537) = new in quality or character, not merely recent — as distinct from νέος (new in time). The newness is demonstrated by its truth being realized: ὅ ἐστιν ἀληθὲς ἐν αὐτῷ καὶ ἐν ὑμῖν — "which is true in him and in you." The command has proved itself real in Christ's life and is now proving itself real in the readers' community. ἡ σκοτία παράγεται καὶ τὸ φῶς τὸ ἀληθινόν ἤδη φαίνει — present tenses: "the darkness is passing away, and the true light is already shining." The command's newness rests on the eschatological overlap: the light is already present.
9: The construction mirrors v.4: a false claim (ὁ λέγων, "the one who says") confronted by behavioral contradiction. ἕως ἄρτι — "until now/still." Even now, in the age of the already-shining light, this person remains in darkness. The present-tense ἐστίν is emphatic: regardless of any verbal claim, this is their present location.
10: ὁ ἀγαπῶν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ἐν τῷ φωτί μένει — "the one who loves his brother abides in the light." Love is the evidence of being in the light — not the cause of it, but the proof. σκάνδαλον ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν — "there is no stumbling block in him." Σκάνδαλον (G4625): originally the trigger of a trap; here, what would cause another to fall. The love-abider is safe not only for themselves but for others.
11: The one who hates is located in three consecutive darkness phrases: ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ ἐστίν (is in the darkness), ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ περιπατεῖ (walks in the darkness), οὐκ οἶδεν ποῦ ὑπάγει (does not know where he is going). The triple pattern is rhetorical — each clause reinforces total disorientation. ἡ σκοτία ἐτύφλωσεν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ — "the darkness has blinded his eyes" (aorist — a completed blinding). The hater has been rendered unable to find a way out.
12: Verses 12–14 form a structured threefold address (children, fathers, young men), repeated at vv.13b-14. ἀφέωνται ὑμῖν αἱ ἁμαρτίαι — "your sins have been forgiven" (perfect passive — a past event with present standing). διὰ τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ — "on account of his name." The name carries the full identity and authority of the person; forgiveness is mediated through the specific person of Jesus, not an abstract principle.
13: τὸν ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — "him who is from the beginning." The fathers have known the pre-existent one — echoing 1:1. The depth of their knowing is proportioned to the duration of the relationship with the one who is eternally prior. τὸν πονηρόν (G4190, "the evil one") — with article, a personal referent: Satan, not abstract evil. The young men have "overcome" (νενικήκατε, perfect) the personal adversary — a completed victory that stands.
14: The tense shifts from γράφω (present "I write," vv.12-13a) to ἔγραψα (aorist "I wrote," vv.13b-14). This is the epistolary aorist — the author writes from the future perspective of the reader, who will receive it as a past communication. It is not a reference to a different earlier letter. ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν ὑμῖν μένει — "the word of God abides in you." The word is not merely known but dwelling (μένω, present) — the same indwelling language applied to God himself in later chapters.
15: μὴ ἀγαπᾶτε τὸν κόσμον — present imperative with negation: "do not make a habit of loving the world." Κόσμος (G2889) — in Johannine usage, the world as organized in opposition to God, not the creation per se (cf. 4:9 where God loves the world and 4:14 where the Son is Savior of the world). ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ πατρός — "the love of the Father" — whether objective (our love for the Father) or subjective (the Father's love flowing in us) — is incompatible with world-love. The verb ἀγαπάω is the same for both loves; the objects define the incompatibility.
16: Three elements of the κόσμος: (1) ἡ ἐπιθυμία τῆς σαρκός — desire generated by the bodily/fallen nature; (2) ἡ ἐπιθυμία τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν — desire triggered by what is seen and coveted; (3) ἡ ἀλαζονεία τοῦ βίου — the arrogant display (ἀλαζονεία, G212) of material possessions and status (βίος = material means of living, distinct from ζωή = spiritual life). All three οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ πατρός — "is not from the Father." The preposition ἐκ traces origin: these are not God-originated desires.
17: ὁ κόσμος παράγεται — "the world is passing away" (present, ongoing). The same verb as 2:8 — the darkness also is passing. Both the cosmos and its craving are in the process of dissolution. ὁ δὲ ποιῶν τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "the one who does the will of God abides into the age." Εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα = "forever" (literally "into the age"). The contrast is stark: the world and its craving are transient; the God-doer has the permanence of divine life.
19: ἐξ ἡμῶν ἐξῆλθαν — "they went out from us." The departure itself is the evidence. ἀλλ᾽ οὐκ ἦσαν ἐξ ἡμῶν — "but they were not of us." The preposition ἐκ (of, from) identifies origin and therefore belonging: to be of the community is to belong to it constitutively, not just numerically. εἰ γὰρ ἐξ ἡμῶν ἦσαν, μεμενήκεισαν ἂν μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — a second-class conditional (contrary to fact): they were not of us, therefore they did not remain. The departure was a disclosure, not a surprise.
21: The verse gives the ground for writing: not ignorance but existing knowledge. πᾶν ψεῦδος ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας οὐκ ἔστιν — "every lie is not from the truth." The double negative pattern is emphatic: no lie whatsoever has its origin in the truth. Lie and truth have different sources (ἐκ), not different qualities of the same stream.
22: ψεύστης revisited. The supreme liar is the one who denies the central christological claim: ὁ ἀρνούμενος ὅτι Ἰησοῦς οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ Χριστός — "the one who denies that Jesus is the Christ." The denial has structural consequences: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἀντίχριστος ὁ ἀρνούμενος τὸν πατέρα καὶ τὸν υἱόν — to deny the Son is simultaneously to deny the Father. The Father–Son relation is not separable: one cannot hold the Father while rejecting the Son.
23: πᾶς ὁ ἀρνούμενος τὸν υἱὸν οὐδὲ τὸν πατέρα ἔχει — "everyone who denies the Son does not even have the Father." Ἔχω here = possessing in a relational sense. The logic is tight: the Father is accessible only through the Son. ὁ ὁμολογῶν τὸν υἱὸν καὶ τὸν πατέρα ἔχει — "the one who confesses the Son has the Father also." Ὁμολογέω here: not just affirming truth but openly declaring allegiance — a public, verbal act.
24:
ὑμεῖς ὃ ἠκούσατε ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — the appeal to "what you heard from the beginning" returns to the letter's opening framework (1:1). The readers' anchor is apostolic testimony received at conversion, not new revelations from departing teachers. ἐν ὑμῖν μενέτω — imperative: "let it remain in you." The readers are commanded to ensure the message stays in residence.

ἐν τῷ υἱῷ καὶ ἐν τῷ πατρί μενεῖτε — "you will remain in the Son and in the Father." The conditional structure: if the message abides in them, they will abide in the divine persons. Doctrinal faithfulness and relational indwelling are inseparable in Johannine theology.
25: ἡ ἐπαγγελία ἥν αὐτὸς ἐπηγγείλατο ἡμῖν — "the promise that he himself promised us." The cognate accusative construction (ἐπαγγελία ... ἐπηγγείλατο) emphasizes the directness: this is exactly what he committed to. τὴν ζωὴν τὴν αἰώνιον — "the eternal life" (definite article, as in 1:2). The promise returns to the letter's opening — the eternal life that was with the Father and was manifested is now the promised destination for those who remain.
26: A transitional verse explaining the foregoing polemic. τῶν πλανώντων ὑμᾶς — "those who lead you astray" (πλανάω, G4105): those who cause to wander. The language of deception and wandering frames the false teachers' activity throughout 1 John. John writes not to correct ignorance but to arm against active, ongoing deceivers.
28: νῦν, τεκνία, μένετε ἐν αὐτῷ — "now, dear children, abide in him." The imperative μένετε pulls together the abiding theme of the chapter. ἵνα ἐὰν φανερωθῇ σχῶμεν παρρησίαν — "so that when he is revealed we may have boldness." Παρρησία (G3954): literally "all-speech" — the freedom of unrestricted speech, here the eschatological confidence of the community at the parousia, contrasted with αἰσχυνθῶμεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "shrink in shame from him." The verb αἰσχύνω connotes the social shame of public exposure.
29: ἐὰν εἰδῆτε ὅτι δίκαιός ἐστιν — "if you know that he is righteous." The condition is not in doubt; the ἐάν introduces an inference. γινώσκετε ὅτι πᾶς ὁ ποιῶν τὴν δικαιοσύνην ἐξ αὐτοῦ γεγέννηται — "know that everyone who practices righteousness has been born of him." Γεγέννηται: perfect passive of γεννάω — "has been born" — a past completed act with present standing. The new birth is the ground of righteous practice; practice is the evidence of birth, not its cause. This verse bridges chapter 2 to chapter 3's extended development of the born-of-God theme.
